name: god-human-animal-machine
description: Meghan O'Gieblyn's God, Human, Animal, Machine — philosophical toolkit examining how technology reshapes our understanding of consciousness, meaning, faith, and humanity in the age of AI
useCase: Understand AI and consciousness, critique transhumanism, examine disenchantment and meaning, and navigate the philosophical questions raised by living in an age of algorithms
knownRisks: The book is a collection of philosophical essays, not a systematic argument — some readers may find it meandering
O'Gieblyn writes from a specific cultural position (ex-evangelical, now secular) that may not resonate with all readers
The critique of technology is philosophical, not practical — no specific advice for limiting screen time or managing digital life
